title: Fagara micrantha
intro: Fagara micrantha, also known as Zanthoxylum micranthum, is a species of plant in the Rutaceae family, commonly referred to as the Prickly Ash tree. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, providing relief from pain, fever, and digestive issues. Bioactive constituents of Fagara micrantha include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Fagara micrantha is often prepared as a tea, infused oil, or dried root powder, and is commonly used to treat a range of ailments, including arthritis, respiratory issues, and gastrointestinal problems.
taxonomy: Fagara micrantha is classified within the Rutaceae family, commonly known as the citrus family. This classification is due to its close relationship with other plants in this family, which are primarily known for their citrus fruits and fragrant leaves. The species Fagara micrantha is further classified as a type of shrub, with small, yellow flowers and dark green, leathery leaves. Its classification within the Rutaceae family is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic similarities with other members of this family. This classification helps to understand the evolutionary relationships between Fagara micrantha and other plants in the Rutaceae family.
